metadata.title: What is Brutalist?
metadata.purpose: The intro video for the 'Brutalist — Claude for Video Production' playlist on @NikBearBrown. An explainer that argues the conductor idea (you decide, the machine plays) and shows the real terminal workflow. Meta: this video is made by the toolkit, about the toolkit.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — the master clock; every beat conforms to its measured audio
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'BrutalistTerminalOpen' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B99: palette=claude but the outro is 'BrutalistCommentCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. This is an experiment. This is 'What is Brutalist?' — the first in a series on making videos with Claude. Every video in it, including this one, was built by the toolkit it describes. More at www dot brutalist dot art.

checklist: fill-in: machine makes what it can

beats.title: What is a review label?
beats.narration_text: One quick thing before we start. You'll notice a small grey label in the corner of every shot. That's the review label. It names each beat — its number, whether it's a Manim animation, a Remotion graphic, or AI video, where it falls on the timeline, and how long it holds. I normally strip it from the finished cut. I'm leaving it in this one, so you can watch the machinery while I explain it.
You can ask an AI to make a whole video in one click. And most of the time, what comes back is slop — clean, rendered, and dead on arrival. Why?

production_viz.label: one-click → 'AI slop' stamp
production_viz.mechanic: a big button labeled 'make video' clicks; a filmstrip drops out fully formed; a red ink stamp lands across it reading 'SLOP'. Clean but lifeless.

beats.narration_text: Here's the asymmetry that explains it. Claude cannot watch the video. It has never sat in the audience.

production_viz.label: an eye, crossed out, facing a screen
production_viz.mechanic: a screen plays a tiny cut; a stylized 'AI' node sits opposite it with a closed/greyed eye and a dashed line to the screen that never connects. The machine renders the screen but cannot receive it.

beats.narration_text: So it can't tell you whether a joke lands, whether an opening is interesting or merely competent, whether the idea actually clicked. Taste isn't a step it does slowly — it's a step it can't do at all.

production_viz.label: funny? interesting? did it land?
production_viz.mechanic: three question cards flip up — 'funny?', 'interesting?', 'did it land?' — each with an empty checkbox the AI node can't fill; a human silhouette reaches over and checks them.

beats.narration_text: But flip it around. Claude is superhuman at the build. It writes Remotion and Manim faster and more correctly than you ever will.

props.caption: the machine's native tongue

beats.narration_text: A human spending twenty hours hunting a render bug is twenty hours thrown away — that's exactly the work the machine is best at. Asking a person to do it is as wasteful as asking Claude to decide what's funny.

production_viz.label: 20 hrs on a render bug → waste
production_viz.mechanic: a clock spins through '20:00:00' while a human hunches over a single red error line; beside it the same fix resolves in a few machine ticks. The wasted-time bar drains.

beats.narration_text: So the two ways to get it wrong sit at the extremes. Push the work onto the human, and you're debugging TypeScript at 2 a.m. to ship ideas that were ready hours ago. Push it onto the machine, and you get slop — because nobody in the audience ever decided it was worth watching.

production_viz.label: time-sink  ⟷  AI slop
production_viz.mechanic: a balance beam: left pan 'all human' sags under a pile of clocks (time-sink); right pan 'all machine' holds the SLOP filmstrip. Both pans are failure. A marker points to the balanced middle.

beats.narration_text: Brutalist holds the line in the middle. And the line has a name: you are the conductor.

production_viz.label: YOU ARE THE CONDUCTOR
production_viz.mechanic: hero card: a conductor's baton draws a single confident stroke across a dark canvas; the title 'YOU ARE THE CONDUCTOR' resolves in EB Garamond; a faint orchestra of tool-icons (Manim, Remotion, ffmpeg) sits below, waiting for the downbeat.

beats.narration_text: You decide what the piece is. You listen for the wrong note. You own the result. The machine — an extraordinary player — performs every part on request, in order, one beat at a time. The score is yours. The playing is its.

production_viz.label: the score is yours · the playing is its
production_viz.mechanic: split frame: left, a hand annotates a score (intent, taste, the wrong note circled in red); right, the tool-orchestra plays those marks into rendered beats. Baton on the left drives the players on the right.

beats.narration_text: Here's that loop — live, from building this very video. Look: that box was too narrow, the text spilled over the edge. I didn't open the code. I just said, 'make the boxes wider to hold the text.' And it did. I catch the wrong note; the machine fixes it.

production_viz.label: before → instruction → after
production_viz.mechanic: show the real narrow-box before (text overflowing, red clipped-edge flag); my instruction appears as a line of text ('make the boxes wider to hold the text'); the box widens smoothly and the text now fits cleanly in ink.

beats.narration_text: Here's how it actually works. Every video is a beat sheet — one beat per moment. That sheet is the heart; everything else is derived from it.

production_viz.label: beat_sheet.json — the heart
production_viz.mechanic: a beat_sheet.json glows at center as a heart; rows B01…Bnn pulse; arrows fan out to derived views (todo.json, STATUS.md, the cut) — one-way, all flowing FROM the sheet.

beats.narration_text: First, fill-in: the machine renders every beat it can make — a Manim animation, a Remotion graphic, a title card. If the machine can build a beat, the machine builds it. You are never asked to hand-make something Claude already knows how to do.

output: [fill-in] 11 pipeline beats filled — 3 human beats remain

beats.narration_text: What's left is only the beats a machine honestly can't make — real footage, an archival image, a performance. For those it leaves a request card, with a suggested prompt. You drop the real thing in the pantry; the tool trims it to the beat and slots it in. A request card on a beat the machine could animate is a bug.

production_viz.label: request card → pantry → conformed cut
production_viz.mechanic: an unfilled beat shows a 'request card' (icon + suggested prompt); a human hand drops a film clip into a 'pantry' bin; the tool auto-trims it to the beat's length and clicks it into the timeline slot.

beats.narration_text: None of this needs a black box. Ask the doctor what you can make right now.

output: previz            ✅ ready   no keys — ffmpeg + Pillow
lyric-resync      ⚠ needs key   higgsfield CLI login

beats.narration_text: Then conduct. List the skills, see what a video still needs, and compile.

props.caption: you conduct — three commands
props.code:
$ art --list                 # every skill
$ art todo   my-video --open  # what each beat needs, and how
$ art run    my-video         # compile; only human beats remain as cards

beats.narration_text: That's Brutalist. The machine does what machines are superhuman at. You do what only an audience can. Every video in this series is one worked example — made by the toolkit, about the toolkit — so you can rebuild it, then point it at your own ideas.

production_viz.label: one playlist · one example per video type
production_viz.mechanic: a playlist rail of thumbnails builds left to right — 'What is Brutalist?', 'sketch-explainer', 'explainer', 'lecture', 'music-video' … each labeled with its ./art command underneath.

beats.narration_text: Subscribe to follow the series, and find the whole toolkit at www dot brutalist dot art. You bring the taste. The machine brings the hands.

props.comment: // you bring the taste — the machine brings the hands
